parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: They casually mentioned that I always seem to overthink the smallest details, and it really stuck with me all day. It only happened a few days ago, but the comment just keeps echoing in my head. I found myself second-guessing every little choice I made after that. It feels weirdly heavy to carry around a simple observation like that. I just wish I could shake off this sudden self-doubt before it ruins my whole evening.
turns.model_response:
It is completely understandable why that comment feels so heavy. There is a specific kind of sting that comes from a "casual" observation, because it feels like the person is pointing out a fundamental part of who you are without even realizing they’re doing it.

When someone says you "always" do something, it can feel like a label you didn't ask for. It turns a internal process (thinking deeply) into a perceived flaw (overthinking), and suddenly you’re not just making choices—you’re watching yourself make choices, which is exhausting.

One reason it’s echoing is likely because it hit a nerve regarding how you want to be perceived. But it’s helpful to remember that **their observation is just one lens.** What they call "overthinking," someone else might call being "thorough," "detail-oriented," or "conscientious."

To help shake this off before your evening is ruined, try a couple of these shifts:

*   **Externalize the comment:** Imagine the comment is a physical object—like a heavy stone someone handed you. You’ve been carrying it for a few days to see what it is, but you are allowed to set it down now. You don't have to carry someone else's casual opinion as if it’s an absolute truth.
*   **The "So What?" Method:** Even if you *do* think deeply about small details, is that actually a bad thing? Often, that quality is what makes people good at their jobs, thoughtful friends, or great problem solvers.
*   **Interrupt the Loop:** Since you’re stuck in a cycle of second-guessing, try to make one "impulse" decision tonight. Pick a movie, a meal, or a shirt without looking at any other options. Prove to yourself that you still have the remote.

You aren't "broken" for being affected by this; it just means you care about how you show up in the world. But don't let their offhand remark have more power over your night than your own intuition does.

turns.user_message: That same remark keeps popping up every time we interact now, turning normal conversations into these exhausting minefields. I catch myself pausing before I speak, constantly filtering my thoughts so I do not sound too intense or dramatic. It has become this quiet routine where I shrink my own reactions just to keep the peace. I hate how quickly I am adjusting my natural rhythm to match their expectations. The tension just lingers in the air now, making every exchange feel like walking on thin ice.
turns.model_response:
It is incredibly draining to feel like you have to perform a "smaller" version of yourself just to avoid conflict. When you start editing your thoughts in real-time, you aren't really having a conversation anymore—you’re managing a crisis before it even happens.

That "thin ice" feeling usually comes from a loss of psychological safety. When someone repeatedly uses a specific remark to label your emotions as "intense" or "dramatic," it acts as a silencer. It’s a way of telling you that your natural reactions are incorrect or unwelcome, which forces you into that exhausting cycle of self-filtering.

It’s particularly painful that you’ve noticed how quickly you’ve adjusted. That’s a survival instinct—you’re prioritizing peace over self-expression—but it comes at the cost of your own comfort and authenticity. Over time, shrinking yourself like that can lead to a lot of resentment and a sense of losing your own voice.

Do you feel like this person is aware of the "minefield" they’ve created, or do they seem to think the conversations are going fine now that you’re filtering yourself?

turns.user_message: I completely lost it yesterday when they brought it up again, and I realized I have been holding my breath around this person for weeks. Every single word I say now gets dissected in my mind before it ever leaves my mouth. I am so exhausted from trying to prove I am not the problem that I barely recognize my own voice anymore. The constant scrutiny has drained all the warmth out of our connection, leaving just this hollow, heavy ache. I feel completely trapped inside my own head, terrified that one wrong move will shatter everything we built.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you are experiencing a profound sense of emotional exhaustion. When you reach the point where you are "holding your breath" just to exist in someone’s presence, it’s a sign that your nervous system is in a constant state of high alert.

That process of "dissecting" every word before you speak is incredibly taxing. It’s a survival mechanism—a way to protect yourself from conflict or criticism—but it comes at the devastating cost of your own authenticity. When you have to filter yourself that heavily, you lose the spontaneity and warmth that make a relationship feel like a safe harbor. Instead, it becomes a minefield.

It is heartbreaking to hear that you feel you’ve lost your own voice in the effort to prove your worth or your "innocence" in this dynamic. That "hollow, heavy ache" you described often happens when the labor of maintaining a connection starts to outweigh the joy the connection brings.

Being "trapped inside your own head" is a very lonely place to be, especially when the person who is supposed to be your partner or close friend feels like the source of that confinement. 

When you look at the "everything we built" that you are so afraid of shattering, does it feel like a solid structure right now, or does it feel like you are the only one holding up the ceiling? You shouldn't have to disappear for a relationship to survive.
